narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 83% chance that you would not pass based on your profile. This high predicted risk of failure means the model flagged your application as too uncertain for admission, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger signals of bar success. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your planned part-time enrollment status, your third-year grade decile in school, and your family's middle income bracket. Being part-time stood out as the strongest influence, as it's much less common among students who pass the bar—only about 7% of them were part-time compared to over 90% full-time—suggesting the model sees it as a major hurdle to the focused preparation needed for success. Your third-year decile of 3 also pushed strongly toward the failure prediction, since passers typically ranked around the 6th decile, indicating your later performance didn't match the consistency the model associates with bar passers. Your middle family income level had a smaller but still negative pull, as it's less frequent among successful students (who are more often from upper-middle or high-income backgrounds) and may signal fewer resources for the intense study demands. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average of bar passers, and while your LSAT of 33 fell short of their 37 average and your first-year decile of 5 was below their 6, these played lesser roles here. Overall, your profile compares to accepted students by lacking the full-time commitment and higher later-year rankings that define most bar successes. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan, as that alone could shift the model's view dramatically, or aim to boost future academic deciles through targeted improvement. Retaking the LSAT for a score closer to 37 or higher could also help counterbalance these factors next time. Many students in similar spots succeed by addressing just one or two key areas like these. You're clearly capable, given your solid undergrad record, so focusing on these changes could open doors soon. Keep pushing—you've got this.
